macro_segments.title: Introduction to Eizehu Mekoman and Outer Chattat Blood Application at Four Corners

micro_segments.title: Opening remarks on Eizehu Mekoman and the structure of outer chattat
Mishna description of blood application: north application, base collection, inner chattat at west base, counterclockwise ascent of altar

macro_segments.title: Debate between Rabbi Yochanan and Rabbi Elazar on Horn Placement and the Definition of Keren (Corner)

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Yochanan's position: amah in each direction from horn is acceptable
Rabbi Elazar's position: application must be at the edge itself (mechatei v'yored k'negged chodo shel keren)
Gemara clarification: debate concerns whether keren extends below horn or only at horn itself

macro_segments.title: Braita Challenge to Amah-on-Each-Side Theory and Reconciliation with Preferred Method

micro_segments.title: Braita describing proper chattat procedure: southeast corner, dipping finger, using thumb and pinky, application opposite horn, using all blood on finger
Gemara resolution: ideal is at edge, but amah-on-each-side acceptable b'dieved

macro_segments.title: Upper and Lower Blood Lines (Damim Elyonim and Damim Tachtonim) and the Red Line Division

micro_segments.title: Introduction to upper bloods (above red line) and lower bloods (below red line); application differs by animal type
Altar structure: one amah base, five amot middle section, four amot upper section, horns; red line at midpoint
Rabbi Elazar b'Rabbi Shimon requires chattat behema at corner itself; Rabbi allows anywhere above red line for bird ola
Derivation of four amot designation from verse: corners take up four amot from top of horn to red line
Red line conceptual purpose: grillwork (reshes) at midpoint divides altar into upper and lower blood applications

macro_segments.title: Remnants of Blood (Shirei HaDam) Placement Debate: South vs. West Base

micro_segments.title: Braita states outer chattat remnants spilled at south base, not west base like inner chattat
Gemara derives south base from logic: come off altar at south, similar to inner chattat coming off from Heichal entrance
Alternative: Rabbi Yishmael says both inner and outer chattaot remnants at west base; Rabbi Shimon Ben Yochai says both at south
Explanation of Rabbi Shimon's position: if entire altar positioned in north, southern doorway exit would face south base
Tradition note: House of Rabbi Yishmael reported Rabbi Shimon's position as west base (majority drew minority)

macro_segments.title: Ola (Burnt Offering) Blood Application: Two Diagonal Corners with Four Applications and Requirement for Protruding Base

micro_segments.title: Mishna: ola is kodshei kodashim; slaughter in north, catch blood in north, two applications that are four, flay, chop, completely burn
Debate on two-that-are-four: Rav says separate application at each side of two corners; Shmuel says one L-shaped (gamma) application hitting both sides
Braita: two-that-are-four interpreted via parallel to chattat's four separate applications (one per wall), not continuous throwing
Application only at two corners (northeast and southwest) because southeast corner lacks protruding base; reason: southeast in Yehuda's portion

macro_segments.title: Tribal Division of Temple Mount: Yehuda and Binyamin Split and the Absence of Southeast Base

micro_segments.title: Yehuda's portion extends into altar area (one amah into Binyamin's section); Binyamin's section is the primary altar location
Explanation via tribal blessings: Binyamin described as devouring wolf; altar positioned to be in Binyamin's portion as his prey
Yehuda's extension into southeast corner area (where base should be) prevents base formation there due to tribal boundary
Midrashic narrative: Binyamin's righteous distress at Yehuda's encroachment led to his merit as God's host (divine presence dwells in his land)
Theological significance: southeast corner lacking base symbolizes Binyamin's portion as place of Shechina dwelling

topical_tags.term: Damim elyonim v'damim tachtonim (upper and lower bloods)
Red line (chut ha-sikra) - midpoint division of altar
Shirei ha-dam (remnants of blood) - base placement
Rabbi Yochanan vs. Rabbi Elazar - amah-on-each-direction debate
Rabbi vs. Rabbi Elazar b'Rabbi Shimon - keren extent below horn
Rabbi Yishmael vs. Rabbi Shimon Ben Yochai - remnant base placement
Rav vs. Shmuel - separate vs. L-shaped blood application
Yesod (protruding base) - requirement for blood application
Southeast corner (keren mizrachit dromit) - lacking base
Tribal divisions - Yehuda and Binyamin portions of Temple Mount
Binyamin as devouring wolf (Binyamin ze'ev yitrof)
